summary: This class revisits the tocify digest pipeline you built in Class 5 and rereads it through the lens of context engineering, showing that its interests file, prompt placeholders, keyword pre-filter, and batching were context engineering all along. It then turns the lens around: how to build a research codebase where good agentic design is present by construction, using three practices — pytest characterization tests, disciplined git commits, and deliberate repository structure — that AI has made cheap enough for any researcher to adopt. Hands-on exercises upgrade your own tocify fork with tests, Conventional Commits, a CLAUDE.md, and a DECISIONS.md.
objectives: Analyze an existing automation like tocify using the vocabulary of context engineering, identifying static context, dynamic injection, pre-processing, and attention-budget scoping in its design
Explain why agentic coding changes the cost-benefit calculus of software engineering practices in research, since agents begin every session without the implicit context in a researcher's head
Map software engineering artifacts to memory categories: tests as executable semantic memory, git history as episodic memory, and repository structure as pre-processed context
Write characterization tests with pytest that freeze a function's current behavior as a tripwire before refactoring or letting an agent modify code
Apply Conventional Commits, a CLAUDE.md, and a DECISIONS.md to a repository so that context is externalized into artifacts agents read automatically
keyTakeaways: The tocify pipeline already embodied context engineering principles: interests.md is static context like a CLAUDE.md, prompt.txt uses dynamic injection points, keyword_prefilter() pre-processes context before it reaches the LLM, and batching in groups of 50 respects attention budgets.

AI coding agents start every session from ignorance, so you either pay a re-explanation tax in every conversation or externalize context once into artifacts the agent reads automatically — making context engineering a property of the codebase itself.

Tests, commit histories, and repository structure are not separate from context engineering; they are context engineering at the timescale of a codebase, a shift practitioners call compound engineering, AI-friendly codebase design, or the context flywheel.

Characterization tests capture a function's current behavior as a tripwire rather than proving theoretical correctness, which is exactly the guarantee you need before refactoring — regressions are the most common way research code produces silently wrong results.

Agents have collapsed the historical entry costs of these practices: they can draft tests, write Conventional Commits messages from a staged diff, and scaffold repository structure, while you contribute review and tacit knowledge.

Research code is not a candidate for vibe coding: because it carries the correctness of your scientific claims, it demands deliberate practices that treat tests, logs, and decision records as first-class outputs.

quiz.question: In the retrospective on tocify, the keyword_prefilter() function — which keeps only the top 200 keyword-matching papers before any LLM call — is identified as an example of which context engineering principle?

options.text: Episodic memory, because it remembers which papers were scored in previous runs
Pre-processing context for consumption, so data is cleaned and scoped to what is relevant before it enters the model's context window
Dynamic injection, because it fills placeholders in prompt.txt at runtime
Instrumentation, because it logs how many papers pass the filter each week

quiz.explanation: The notes describe keyword_prefilter() as implementing 'pre-processing context for consumption': without it, every fetched paper would be sent to the API, wasting tokens and money on irrelevant articles. Instrumentation is actually the one principle tocify fails to implement.

quiz.question: You are about to ask an AI agent to refactor a vorticity function that you trust based on past runs. According to the notes, what is the primary guarantee a characterization test gives you in this situation?

options.text: It proves the function is theoretically correct against the governing equations
It documents the function's API so the agent can call it correctly
It ensures the refactored version will run faster than the original
It freezes the function's current behavior so any silent change — like a flipped sign convention — fails the test immediately

quiz.explanation: The notes are explicit that a characterization test 'does not prove that compute_vorticity is correct; it proves that whatever the function produced on the day you wrote the test, it will continue to produce' — surfacing regressions before they contaminate results.

quiz.question: The notes map software engineering practices onto the memory categories from Class 10. Which pairing matches the mapping given in the lecture?

options.text: A git log with descriptive commit messages is episodic memory: it tells the agent what was done, why, and in what order
A pytest suite is episodic memory: it records the history of bugs the project has encountered
A DECISIONS.md is dynamic context: it is injected only for the duration of a single task
A well-organized repository is semantic memory: it specifies expected behavior in a verifiable form

quiz.explanation: Per the notes, the git log is episodic memory, a pytest suite is executable semantic memory, a well-organized repository is pre-processed context, and DECISIONS.md is static context that prevents the agent from relitigating settled questions.

quiz.question: The lecture argues that the old calculus — where PIs could reasonably dismiss tests, clean commits, and repository structure as overhead — is no longer correct. What is the mechanical reason given for this change?

options.text: Journals and funding agencies now require test suites and version control for published research code
Modern research codebases are too large for any single student to understand without documentation

Agents begin each session without the researcher's accumulated implicit context, so you either re-explain it every conversation or externalize it once into artifacts that pay dividends across all future sessions
AI-generated code contains more bugs than human-written code, so stricter review processes are needed

quiz.explanation: The notes frame this as a choice between paying a per-conversation re-explanation tax and treating context engineering as a property of the codebase itself; meanwhile, agents have collapsed the entry cost of the practices by drafting tests, commit messages, and scaffolding.

quiz.question: Why do the notes argue that 'vibe coding' — giving an agent high-level intent and iterating until the output feels right — is inappropriate for research code, even though it has legitimate uses?

options.text: Vibe coding only works with the largest frontier models, which most research groups cannot afford
Research code carries the correctness of scientific claims, where a sign error that 'felt right' and passed visual inspection can invalidate a thesis chapter
Vibe coding is prohibited by university academic integrity policies
Research code changes too frequently for an iterative, feel-based workflow to keep up

quiz.explanation: The notes grant vibe coding a legitimate place for throwaway scripts and prototypes where correctness is negotiable, but research code demands deliberate practices — tests, decision records, explanatory commits — because its correctness underwrites scientific claims.
